stem: Find the range of this dataset: $3, \ 7, \ 12, \ 5, \ 9$
explanation: The highest value is $12$ and the lowest is $3$, so the range $= 12 - 3 = 9$.

stem: Find the range of this dataset: $15, \ 8, \ 23, \ 11, \ 19$
explanation: The highest value is $23$ and the lowest is $8$, so the range $= 23 - 8 = 15$.

stem: Find the range of this dataset: $42, \ 38, \ 51, \ 29, \ 45$
explanation: The highest value is $51$ and the lowest is $29$, so the range $= 51 - 29 = 22$.

stem: Find the range of this dataset: $100, \ 75, \ 88, \ 62, \ 95$
explanation: The highest value is $100$ and the lowest is $62$, so the range $= 100 - 62 = 38$.

stem: Find the interquartile range (IQR) of this ordered dataset: $2, \ 5, \ 7, \ 10, \ 13, \ 16, \ 20$
hint: Split the data about the median. $Q_1$ is the median of the lower half and $Q_3$ is the median of the upper half. $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$.
explanation: There are 7 values, so the median is the 4th value: $10$. The lower half is $\{2, 5, 7\}$, giving $Q_1 = 5$. The upper half is $\{13, 16, 20\}$, giving $Q_3 = 16$. $\text{IQR} = 16 - 5 = 11$.

stem: Find the interquartile range (IQR) of this ordered dataset: $4, \ 8, \ 12, \ 16, \ 20, \ 24$
hint: For an even number of values, split directly into two equal halves. $Q_1$ is the median of the lower half and $Q_3$ is the median of the upper half.
explanation: There are 6 values. The lower half is $\{4, 8, 12\}$, giving $Q_1 = 8$. The upper half is $\{16, 20, 24\}$, giving $Q_3 = 20$. $\text{IQR} = 20 - 8 = 12$.

stem: Find the interquartile range (IQR) of this ordered dataset: $3, \ 6, \ 8, \ 12, \ 16, \ 20, \ 24, \ 28$
hint: Split the 8 values into two groups of 4. Find the median of each group.
explanation: There are 8 values. The lower half is $\{3, 6, 8, 12\}$, so $Q_1 = \dfrac{6 + 8}{2} = 7$. The upper half is $\{16, 20, 24, 28\}$, so $Q_3 = \dfrac{20 + 24}{2} = 22$. $\text{IQR} = 22 - 7 = 15$.

stem: Find the interquartile range (IQR) of this ordered dataset: $1, \ 3, \ 7, \ 9, \ 13, \ 17, \ 21, \ 25, \ 29$
hint: There are 9 values. The median is the 5th value. Split the remaining values into a lower half and an upper half of 4 values each.
explanation: There are 9 values, so the median is the 5th value: $13$. The lower half is $\{1, 3, 7, 9\}$, so $Q_1 = \dfrac{3 + 7}{2} = 5$. The upper half is $\{17, 21, 25, 29\}$, so $Q_3 = \dfrac{21 + 25}{2} = 23$. $\text{IQR} = 23 - 5 = 18$.

stem: A dataset is: $4, \ 7, \ 9, \ 11, \ 13, \ 58$. Find the range.
hint: Range = maximum − minimum. Notice that $58$ is much larger than the other values.
explanation: The maximum is $58$ and the minimum is $4$, so the range $= 58 - 4 = 54$. The outlier $58$ greatly inflates the range.

stem: The value $58$ is an outlier and is removed from the dataset $4, \ 7, \ 9, \ 11, \ 13, \ 58$. What is the range of the remaining data?
explanation: Removing $58$ leaves $\{4, 7, 9, 11, 13\}$. The range $= 13 - 4 = 9$. The range dropped from $54$ to $9$ — a large change caused by one outlier.

hint: Split the 8 values into two halves of 4. The outlier $100$ sits in the upper half — check how much it affects $Q_3$.
explanation: Lower half: $\{10, 12, 14, 16\}$, so $Q_1 = \dfrac{12 + 14}{2} = 13$. Upper half: $\{18, 20, 22, 100\}$, so $Q_3 = \dfrac{20 + 22}{2} = 21$. $\text{IQR} = 21 - 13 = 8$. Compare this with the range of $90$ — the IQR is much less affected by the outlier $100$.

stem: A dataset contains an extreme outlier. Which measure of spread is the better choice to describe the spread of the data, and why?
options: The range, because it uses all values in the dataset
The IQR, because it focuses on the middle 50% of the data and is not affected by extreme values
The range, because it is easier to calculate
The IQR, because it always gives a smaller value than the range
answer: The IQR, because it focuses on the middle 50% of the data and is not affected by extreme values
hint: Think about which values each measure uses. The range uses only the minimum and maximum; the IQR ignores the top and bottom quarters.
explanation: The IQR measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data, so an outlier in the top or bottom quarter has little or no effect on it. The range, by contrast, uses only the minimum and maximum, so a single extreme value can make the range very large and misleading.
